title: UGC Creators: The Complete Guide for Brands in 2025
deck: Here’s the thing: 90% of consumers say they want to see content from real customers. Not actors. Not models. Real people.
heroAlt: Consumer Preference Real Customer Content chart

blocks.text: Still paying $10K+ for polished brand content that nobody believes?
Here’s the thing: 90% of consumers say they want to see content from real customers. Not actors. Not models. Real people.
These aren’t your typical influencers chasing follower counts. UGC creators make authentic, relatable content that looks like it came from your customers—because stylistically, it does.
Let’s break down everything you need to know about working with UGC creators in 2025.
A UGC creator is someone who produces user-generated content style videos and photos for brands—but they’re not necessarily posting it to their own audience.
That’s the key difference from traditional influencers.
Traditional influencer: Creates content AND distributes it to their followers UGC creator: Creates content that brands use in their own marketing
Think of UGC creators as content production partners. They make stuff that feels authentic—unboxings, testimonials, tutorials, day-in-my-life content—and you use it however you want.
You know that overly-polished brand content that screams “AD”? UGC is the opposite.
This isn’t about low quality. It’s about authenticity. And authenticity converts.
Why Brands Are Obsessed With UGC Creators

items: 91.4% increase in conversion rates when UGC appears on product pages
4x higher click-through rates on UGC ads vs. brand-created content
86% of consumers trust brands that prioritize UGC over traditional influencer content

blocks.alt: Consumer Preference Real Customer Content chart

blocks.text: When someone sees a polished brand ad, their brain immediately categorizes it as “selling something.” Guard goes up.
When they see someone who looks like them—using a product in their real life—it registers differently. It’s social proof. It’s relatable. It bypasses the “ad filter.”

blocks.alt: Ugc Performance Conversion Rate Increase chart

blocks.text: Unboxing videos “Look what just arrived!” First impressions, packaging reactions, initial thoughts.
Product reviews/testimonials Honest opinions about features, quality, and value. The good AND the “here’s what could be better.”
Tutorials/How-tos Demonstrating how to use the product. “Let me show you my routine with…”
Day-in-my-life content Product integrated naturally into daily activities. Less “here’s a product” and more “here’s my life (and this product is in it).”
Before/after content Transformation-focused. Skincare, fitness, home organization—anything with visible results.
Problem/solution videos “I had this problem… then I found this product.” Story-driven content.

Genuinely authentic (they actually use your product)

blocks.text: Facebook groups, Discord servers, and Reddit communities dedicated to UGC creators.
What to Look for in a UGC Creator
Not all UGC creators are created equal. Here’s what separates the good from the great:

items: Content that feels natural, not scripted
Good audio quality (this matters more than video quality)
Ability to match different brand aesthetics
Range of content types (not just one style)
Content aesthetic (does it match your brand?)
Portfolios full of only one brand (lack of range)

blocks.text: How to Work With UGC Creators
Before reaching out, get clear on:
Content type: Video? Photo? Both? Quantity: How many pieces per creator? Usage: Where will you use this content? Timeline: When do you need deliverables? Budget: What can you spend per piece?

items: Video dimensions (9:16 for TikTok/Reels, 1:1 for feed)
How many rounds of revisions are included?
What’s the turnaround time for feedback?

blocks.text: Pro tip: Give direction, not scripts. The best UGC comes when creators have room to be themselves.

items: You need high-volume content for ads
You want full control over distribution
Budget is limited but content needs are high
You need access to an established audience
Brand awareness is the primary goal
You want organic reach and engagement
Building long-term creator relationships is the strategy

blocks.text: Many brands use a hybrid strategy:

items: UGC creators for ad content and owned channels

blocks.text: “Say exactly this…” kills authenticity. Give talking points and key messages, but let creators speak naturally.
Bad audio ruins good video. Make sure creators have decent microphones or shoot in quiet environments.
Get usage rights in writing before using content. This protects both parties.
UGC creators aren’t influencers. Don’t expect them to post to their audience or drive direct sales.
When you find great UGC creators, keep working with them. Consistency builds efficiency and better content over time.
For brands serious about UGC, build a system:
Creator roster: Build a bench of 10-20 reliable UGC creators across different demographics and styles.
Content calendar: Plan UGC needs quarterly, not ad-hoc.
Brief templates: Standardize your briefing process for efficiency.
Asset library: Organize all UGC content with proper tagging and usage rights documentation.
Performance tracking: Monitor which creators’ content performs best in ads and double down.

AI-assisted creation: Tools helping creators edit faster and improve quality Shoppable UGC: Direct purchase integration within UGC content Live UGC: Real-time product demonstrations and Q&As Community-driven: Brands building private creator communities for ongoing content

blocks.text: Finding influencers is easy. Finding the RIGHT influencers? That’s where most brands struggle.

You can spend hours scrolling through Instagram, building spreadsheets, and reaching out to creators—only to realize they don’t actually fit your brand, their engagement is fake, or they’re already working with your competitor.
Influencer discovery is the foundation of every successful campaign. Get it wrong, and nothing else matters.
Let’s break down how to find influencers who will actually move the needle for your brand.
Influencer discovery is the process of finding, evaluating, and selecting creators who align with your brand goals, target audience, and campaign objectives.
It’s more than just finding popular accounts. True discovery means identifying:

items: Creators whose audience matches your target customer
Authentic voices that align with your brand values

Partners who will genuinely connect with your product

❌ Audience demographics — You can’t see if followers match your target customer ❌ Authentic engagement metrics — Hard to distinguish real engagement from bots ❌ Fake follower detection — No way to verify audience quality ❌ Historical performance — Can’t see engagement trends over time ❌ Contact information — Have to message through Instagram DMs ❌ Systematic discovery — Everything is manual and time-consuming ❌ Rising talent identification — You find who’s already visible, not who’s emerging

items: Discovery time: Under 1 hour per campaign

When to Upgrade to Paid Tools
Here are the signals that it’s time to invest:
If you’re spending 10+ hours per month on manual discovery, a $99-$249/month tool likely saves money overall.
If campaigns underperform because you’re working with creators who looked good superficially but had the wrong audience or fake engagement, better tools pay for themselves.
One campaign per quarter? Free methods work. Weekly influencer partnerships? You need systematic tools.
Free methods find established creators. Tools with real-time scanning find emerging creators before they’re on everyone’s radar—which means better rates and exclusive partnerships.
If competitors consistently partner with creators you’ve never found, they’re using better discovery tools.

items: Understands what your brand actually needs

Free methods show you what’s publicly visible. Gemz scans TikTok, Instagram, and YouTube continuously, surfacing:

items: What creators are posting now (not months ago)
Rising talent before they blow up

Before every manual discovery session, use this checklist:

Preparation: - [ ] Clear campaign goals defined - [ ] Target creator profile documented (niche, size, aesthetic) - [ ] Research account set up (if using Explore method) - [ ] Hashtag list prepared - [ ] Competitor list ready

Discovery: - [ ] Hashtag research completed (at least 10 specific hashtags) - [ ] Explore page trained and analyzed - [ ] Top 3 competitors audited - [ ] Comment sections mined (at least 5 popular posts) - [ ] Instagram search completed - [ ] Known creator networks mapped

Documentation: - [ ] All potential creators logged in spreadsheet - [ ] Basic vetting completed (check for real engagement) - [ ] Content quality assessed - [ ] Notes on why each creator fits - [ ] Ready for outreach
Can I really find good Instagram influencers without paying?

Yes. The methods in this guide work and can surface quality creators. The trade-off is time—free discovery takes 5-10x longer than paid tools. For occasional campaigns with tight budgets, free methods are viable. For ongoing programs, the time cost usually exceeds tool costs.
How do I verify an influencer is legitimate without paid tools?

Look for: consistent posting history (not sudden spikes), comment quality (real sentences vs. generic emojis), follower growth that appears organic (no sudden jumps), and content engagement that matches follower count. Red flags include: massive follower counts with minimal comments, generic or bot-like comments, and inconsistent posting followed by partnership content.
What’s the best free method for finding micro-influencers?

Comment section mining and hashtag research (using specific, niche hashtags) are most effective for micro-influencers. Explore page discovery tends to surface larger accounts. Micro-influencers often engage actively in their communities, making comment analysis particularly valuable.
How many creators should I contact for one campaign?

Expect 15-30% response rates and 10-20% acceptance rates. For a campaign needing 5 creators, build an outreach list of 30-50. Free methods make building lists this size time-consuming, which is where paid tools provide significant efficiency gains.
Is Instagram Creator Marketplace a good free option?

Instagram’s native Creator Marketplace (for business accounts) can be useful, but it’s limited to creators who opt in and requires a business account. Coverage is incomplete compared to full-platform discovery tools.
When should I start using paid tools like Gemz?

When any of these are true: discovery takes more than 4-5 hours per campaign, you’re running more than one campaign per month, campaign performance suggests you’re not finding the best creators, or you need to discover rising talent before competitors.
You don’t need expensive tools to find your first Instagram influencers. The free methods in this guide have helped countless brands launch successful campaigns.
Start with: 1. Hashtag research — Your first 10 specific, niche hashtags 2. Competitor auditing — Who are your top 3 competitors working with? 3. Comment mining — Check 5 popular posts in your space
Build your first list. Run your first campaign. Learn what works.
When you’re ready to scale—when discovery time becomes a bottleneck—upgrade to smarter tools.
AI-powered discovery. Real-time data. Find creators who actually fit your brand.

blocks.text: TikTok has over 1 billion monthly active users. Finding influencers isn’t the problem.
Finding the right TikTok influencers—creators in your specific niche whose audience actually matches your target customer—that’s where most brands struggle.

This guide covers exactly how to find TikTok influencers by niche, from free manual methods to AI-powered discovery tools. You’ll learn what actually works, what wastes time, and how to build a discovery process that surfaces creators who convert.
Why Niche Matters More Than Follower Count
Before diving into methods, let’s establish why niche targeting is so critical.

Niche creators outperform generic influencers: - 3-5x higher engagement rates in their specific category - 2x better conversion rates on sponsored content - 60% lower cost per acquisition - Significantly higher comment quality and relevance
A 30K follower creator in your exact niche will typically outperform a 300K follower creator in a loosely related space. Every time.

Audience intent: When someone follows a niche creator, they care specifically about that topic. A follower of a “budget meal prep” creator is interested in budget meal prep—not just “food” in general.
Trust concentration: Niche creators build deep trust with their audience on specific topics. That trust transfers to product recommendations within their expertise.
Algorithm alignment: TikTok’s algorithm is exceptionally good at putting content in front of interested users. Niche creators benefit from this—their content reaches the most relevant audiences.
Cost efficiency: Niche creators are often overlooked by brands focused on follower counts, meaning better rates and more negotiating flexibility.
Most brands approach TikTok influencer discovery wrong:

This guide explains why influencer databases show wrong data, how it affects your campaigns, and what alternatives exist for brands that need accurate, current information.
The Fundamental Problem with Influencer Databases
Most influencer marketing platforms—including industry leaders—work the same way:

items: Scrape creator profiles from social platforms
Store that data in a searchable database
Update the database periodically (weekly, monthly, or quarterly)
Serve that stored data to users

blocks.text: This approach has a fundamental flaw: social media changes faster than databases update.

items: They capture publicly available information (followers, engagement, bio, recent posts)
This data gets processed and stored
Users search against this stored snapshot

blocks.text: The problem: The moment data is captured, it starts decaying. What was true when scraped may not be true when you search—days, weeks, or months later.
Let’s look at what changes and how quickly:

blocks.alt: Influencer Data Freshness Comparison chart

blocks.text: Here’s what different platforms typically offer:
Premium enterprise platforms: Weekly or bi-weekly updates Standard tools: Monthly updates Budget tools: Quarterly updates or “continuous” (which often means irregular)
Even weekly updates mean data can be 7+ days old when you’re making decisions. In TikTok time, that’s ancient.
Real Costs of Inaccurate Influencer Data
Stale data isn’t just annoying—it has measurable business impact.
The scenario: You build a list of 50 creators based on database information. You craft personalized outreach. You wait for responses.

What happens with stale data: - 15% have inactive emails or have changed management - 20% have pivoted away from your niche - 10% are already exclusive with competitors - 15% have significantly different metrics than shown
Net result: 40-60% of your outreach effort is wasted. Your “50 creator” list is effectively 20-30 viable options.
The scenario: You selected creators with 4%+ engagement rates in your target niche. You negotiated contracts. You launched.

items: Three creators now have 1.5% engagement (algorithm changed, they haven’t adapted)
Two creators pivoted to different content types
One creator’s audience skews 10 years older than shown

blocks.text: Net result: Campaign underperforms. Leadership questions influencer marketing. Future budgets get cut.
When data is wrong, you pay for inefficiency:

Discovery time: Instead of 2 hours, you spend 6+ hours vetting inaccurate lists Outreach cycles: Failed outreach means more rounds of prospecting Partnership failures: Wrong-fit creators waste campaign budget Opportunity cost: While fixing problems, competitors capture better partnerships
Missed rising talent: Databases capture established creators. Rising talent doesn’t appear until after they’ve already grown—when rates are higher and availability is limited.
Following instead of leading: When everyone uses the same stale databases, everyone finds the same creators. No competitive advantage.
Why This Problem Keeps Getting Worse
The influencer database problem isn’t getting better. It’s getting worse.
Rapid pivots are normal: Creators used to build careers in single niches. Now, pivoting is common—sometimes monthly—based on trends, algorithm changes, or personal interests.

Multi-platform presence: Creators manage TikTok, Instagram, YouTube, and more. Activity levels shift between platforms constantly. A creator “active” on Instagram may have moved their focus to TikTok.
Algorithm sensitivity: Platform algorithm changes can crater engagement overnight. A creator’s metrics from last month may be irrelevant this month.
Social platforms are making data access harder:
API restrictions: Instagram, TikTok, and YouTube have all tightened API access, making comprehensive scraping more difficult and less reliable.
Rate limits: Platforms limit how frequently data can be pulled, forcing longer update cycles.
Data availability: Some metrics that were once public are now hidden or restricted.
This means databases are working with less data, updated less frequently, while the underlying reality changes faster than ever.
As the influencer marketing industry grows:
More creators to track: Database platforms try to cover millions of profiles. Quality suffers at scale.
Update prioritization: Platforms prioritize updating high-profile creators. Micro and nano influencers—often the highest-ROI partnerships—get neglected.
Cost pressures: Comprehensive, frequent updates are expensive. Platforms cut corners to maintain margins.
How to Identify Inaccurate Database Data
Before trusting any influencer database, verify its accuracy.
The test: 1. Search for 10 creators you’d consider partnering with 2. Visit their actual profiles on each platform 3. Compare database info to current reality

items: Engagement on recent posts (should approximate database metrics)
More than 10% variance on follower counts
Database shows recent activity, but no posts in 2+ weeks
Engagement dramatically different from displayed metrics
Creators who said “I don’t do that anymore”
Creators with significantly different current metrics
Any category significantly higher than healthy rates

blocks.text: The test: 1. Build a list from your database 2. Verify the list manually within 24 hours 3. Wait 2 weeks 4. Verify again
What you’ll learn: How quickly your database’s data decays, and whether update frequency is adequate for your needs.
The fundamental problem with databases is temporal: they capture moments, not reality.
The alternative is real-time discovery—platforms that scan social media continuously and surface current information when you search.
Instead of storing and serving cached data:

items: Continuous scanning: Platforms like Gemz scan TikTok, Instagram, and YouTube continuously
On-demand analysis: When you search, results reflect current content and metrics
Live ranking: AI analyzes current relevance, not cached snapshots
Fresh results: You see what creators are doing now, not last month

blocks.text: Data accuracy: Information reflects current reality, not historical snapshots.
Rising talent discovery: New and emerging creators appear as they gain traction—not after they’re already established.
Niche accuracy: Pivots are immediately visible. You won’t outreach to creators who’ve moved on.
Engagement reality: Current engagement metrics, not cached numbers that may no longer apply.
Contact validity: More likely to reach creators through current information.
Gemz combines real-time scanning with AI-powered relevance ranking:

items: Results based on current content and activity
Understanding of actual content topics, not just keywords
Ranking by genuine fit, not just metrics
TikTok, Instagram, and YouTube in one search

blocks.text: When Database Tools Still Make Sense
To be fair, databases aren’t always wrong for every use case.
If you need to understand how a creator’s metrics have changed over time, databases with historical data provide value that real-time tools don’t.
Databases tracking brand partnerships can show historical competitor activity that point-in-time scans miss.
For agencies managing many existing relationships, database-style CRMs help track partnership history.
Using database tools as a secondary verification source (alongside real-time discovery) can provide useful context.

items: Real-time tools for discovery and current verification
Database tools for historical context and relationship management

blocks.text: Switching from Database to Real-Time Discovery

items: Run the spot-check tests described above
Document time spent on discovery and vetting

blocks.text: This establishes a baseline for comparison.

items: Sign up for Gemz or similar real-time tool
Run same campaign searches through both
Compare results: accuracy, relevance, time spent
Campaign performance by creator discovery method

blocks.text: Real-time discovery typically shows improvement within 1-2 campaign cycles.

items: Build new workflows around real-time discovery

blocks.text: Always-verify approach: Never outreach without manual verification of:
Verification timing: Verify within 48 hours of outreach. Data older than that may already be stale.

items: Log database accuracy issues when discovered
Note which data points are most frequently wrong

blocks.text: This data supports tool evaluation and process improvement.
Learn from failures: When outreach fails due to data issues:
How often should influencer data be updated to be reliable?

For most use cases, data older than 1 week has significant accuracy risks. TikTok data specifically can become stale within days due to rapid algorithm and trend changes. Real-time scanning eliminates this concern entirely.
Can I trust any influencer database’s data?

All database-style tools have the same fundamental limitation: they capture snapshots that decay. Some are better than others—premium tools with more frequent updates and larger infrastructure will be more accurate—but none can match real-time scanning for current accuracy.
What’s the most common inaccurate data point in influencer databases?

Engagement rate is typically the most misleading metric. It changes constantly based on content performance, algorithm shifts, and posting frequency. Follower counts are actually more stable than most other metrics.
How do I know if a creator has pivoted niches?

Check their last 10-15 posts manually. If the content doesn’t match what their database profile suggests, they’ve likely pivoted. Real-time tools catch this automatically; databases often lag by weeks or months.
Is real-time discovery more expensive than database tools?

Not necessarily. Gemz starts at $99/month—comparable to many database tools. When you factor in time savings and improved campaign performance from better data, real-time tools often deliver better ROI.
Should I stop using influencer databases entirely?

For primary discovery, real-time tools are superior. But databases can still provide value for historical analysis, relationship management, and secondary verification. Many teams use both strategically.
The influencer database model is fundamentally flawed. Data captured yesterday might not reflect reality today, and data captured last month is almost certainly wrong.
Every campaign built on inaccurate data risks:
Real-time discovery solves this by showing you what’s actually happening—not what happened when someone last ran a scraper.
Real-time scanning. AI-powered relevance. Data you can actually trust.
Find creators based on who they are today, not who they were last month.

blocks.text: So you’ve been scrolling through LinkedIn, watching brands collaborate with creators, and thinking… “Wait, people get PAID to do this?”
Yep. Influencer marketing jobs are legit—and they’re growing fast.
The influencer marketing industry hit $21.1 billion in 2023 and shows no signs of slowing down. That means brands need people to manage it all. People like you, potentially.
Let’s break down everything you need to know about landing an influencer marketing job in 2025.

blocks.alt: Influencer Marketing Industry Size 21 Billion chart

blocks.text: Influencer marketing jobs involve connecting brands with content creators to promote products and services. But that’s the oversimplified version.
In reality, these roles span a wide range of responsibilities:

items: Finding and vetting influencers (this is where it gets interesting)
Managing campaigns from start to finish
Analyzing ROI and reporting to stakeholders

blocks.text: Some people specialize in one area. Others do it all. The industry is still young enough that roles are constantly evolving.
The most common title you’ll see. These folks own the entire influencer strategy for a brand or agency.
Entry-level position. Perfect for breaking into the field.
You work FOR the influencers, not the brands.

items: Build relationships with brands and agencies

blocks.text: Senior leadership role. You’re setting the vision.

items: Own the entire influencer marketing function
Manage teams of coordinators and managers

blocks.text: Skills You Need for Influencer Marketing Jobs

items: Social media platform expertise — You need to LIVE on TikTok, Instagram, and YouTube
Data analysis — Understanding engagement rates, CPM, and ROI calculations
Project management — Juggling 20+ influencer partnerships simultaneously
Influencer discovery tools — Knowing how to find creators efficiently (platforms like Gemz make this way easier with AI-powered discovery)

Content strategy — Understanding what performs on each platform
Relationship building — Influencers are people, not billboards
Trend awareness — Spotting what’s next before it blows up
Flexibility — Campaigns change. Influencers flake. You adapt.
Creativity — Coming up with campaign concepts that don’t feel like ads

blocks.text: Where to Find Influencer Marketing Jobs

items: LinkedIn — Filter by “influencer marketing” in marketing roles
Built In — Great for startup roles
We Work Remotely — Many influencer marketing roles are remote-friendly

blocks.text: How to Break Into Influencer Marketing (Even With No Experience)
Run your own social accounts. Seriously. Grow an Instagram or TikTok to 1,000+ followers. You’ll learn more from doing than any course.
Freelance for small brands. Offer to manage influencer outreach for a local business. You’ll build a portfolio fast.
Intern. Many agencies offer paid internships. It’s the classic foot-in-the-door approach.

items: Follow industry newsletters (like Lia Haberman’s ICYMI)
Listen to podcasts (Creator Upload, Earned by Traackr)
Get certified (HubSpot, Meta, Google have free courses)
Case studies from any influencer work you’ve done
Your own social media growth story

blocks.text: Day in the Life: Influencer Marketing Manager
Here’s what a typical day might look like:
9:00 AM — Check emails. Three influencers need content approved. One needs a deadline extension. Handle it.
10:00 AM — Discovery time. Search for creators for Q2 campaigns using your influencer discovery platform. AI tools like Gemz help you find creators faster than manual scrolling.
11:00 AM — Team meeting. Review last week’s campaign performance. Engagement was up 23%—nice.
12:00 PM — Lunch. Scroll TikTok “for research.” (It counts.)
1:00 PM — Contract negotiations with a mid-tier creator. They want $5K; budget is $3.5K. Find middle ground.
2:30 PM — Brief creation for upcoming campaign. Write creative guidelines that are clear but not restrictive.
4:00 PM — Reporting. Pull metrics for the monthly exec presentation.
5:00 PM — Relationship maintenance. DM a few creators to check in. No ask—just building rapport.

The Future of Influencer Marketing Careers
The field is evolving fast. Here’s where it’s headed:
More specialized roles — We’re seeing titles like “TikTok Partnership Manager” and “Creator Economy Strategist” emerge.

Performance focus — Brands want ROI. Roles increasingly require strong analytics skills.
Creator-side opportunities — More people are building careers helping creators monetize, not just brands find them.

The Big Picture: Industry Growth in 2025

items: $24 billion — Projected global influencer marketing spend in 2025
67% of marketers plan to increase their influencer budgets this year
TikTok overtook Instagram as the #1 platform for influencer campaigns

blocks.alt: Brand Investment Influencer Marketing Growth chart

blocks.text: The industry isn’t just growing—it’s maturing. Brands are getting smarter about ROI, creators are professionalizing their businesses, and the tech powering it all is evolving rapidly.
1. TikTok’s Creator Fund Gets a Major Overhaul
TikTok completely revamped its creator monetization program in late 2024, and the effects are still rippling through the industry.

items: The original Creator Fund (notoriously low payouts) is being phased out
New “Creativity Program” pays significantly more—up to 20x higher per view
Minimum requirements: 10K followers, 100K views in 30 days

blocks.text: Why it matters: More creators can now earn sustainable income directly from TikTok. This means:

items: Less reliance on brand deals for mid-tier creators
Higher rates for sponsored content (creators can afford to be picky)

blocks.text: 2. Instagram Doubles Down on Broadcast Channels
Instagram’s Broadcast Channels feature—one-to-many messaging for creators—is becoming a major influencer marketing opportunity.

items: Over 500 million users now follow at least one Broadcast Channel
Brands are partnering with creators for exclusive channel content
New features allow polls, voice messages, and collaborative posts

blocks.text: For marketers: Broadcast Channels offer something rare: direct access to highly engaged followers without algorithm interference. Smart brands are securing early partnerships here.
3. FTC Cracks Down on Undisclosed Partnerships
The Federal Trade Commission isn’t playing around anymore.

items: Multiple high-profile influencers received warning letters
Brands are being held equally responsible for disclosure violations
New guidelines specifically address AI-generated content and virtual influencers
Every paid partnership needs clear disclosure (#ad, #sponsored, or platform tools) - “Gifted” products still require disclosure
AI influencer content has specific requirements

blocks.text: (We cover this in detail in our FTC Influencer Marketing Compliance Guide.)

LinkedIn launched a dedicated creator fund - “Thought leader ads” let brands boost influencer content natively

blocks.text: For brands: YouTube Shorts offers a massive reach opportunity with better monetization infrastructure than TikTok. Expect to see more creator attention—and higher rates—on this platform.
